concepts.title: Linear Equations and Inequalities
concepts.content_html:
Linear Equations and Inequalities on the SAT

The SAT Math section tests your ability to solve linear equations and inequalities quickly and accurately. These questions make up a large portion of the Heart of Algebra domain — roughly 8 out of 58 math questions.

Key Concept
A linear equation is any equation that can be written in the form \(ax + b = c\), where \(a\), \(b\), and \(c\) are constants and \(x\) is the variable.

SAT Strategy: Translate Words into Math

Many SAT linear equation problems are word problems. The key skill is translating English into algebra:

  • "is" or "equals" → \(=\)
  • "more than" or "increased by" → \(+\)
  • "less than" or "decreased by" → \(-\)
  • "times" or "product of" → \(\times\)
  • "per" or "for each" → division or multiplication depending on context

Solving Linear Inequalities

Linear inequalities follow the same rules as equations with one critical difference:

Watch Out!
When you multiply or divide both sides of an inequality by a negative number, you must flip the inequality sign.
Example: \(-2x > 6\) becomes \(x < -3\) after dividing by \(-2\).

Absolute Value Equations

The SAT occasionally tests absolute value equations. Remember:

If \(|ax + b| = c\) where \(c > 0\), then:

\[ax + b = c \quad \text{or} \quad ax + b = -c\]

SAT Tip
Always check your answers by plugging them back into the original equation. On the SAT, wrong answer choices are designed to match common calculation errors — substitution catches these mistakes.

No-Solution and Infinite-Solution Equations

The SAT loves testing whether an equation has no solution, one solution, or infinitely many solutions:

  • No solution: simplifying leads to a false statement like \(5 = 3\)
  • One solution: simplifying leads to \(x = \text{some number}\)
  • Infinitely many solutions: simplifying leads to a true identity like \(0 = 0\)
concepts.content_raw: Linear Equations and Inequalities on the SAT

problems.question_html: A gym charges a one-time registration fee of $25 plus $40 per month. If Marcus has budgeted $345 for gym expenses, which equation can be used to find the maximum number of months, \(m\), he can afford?
problems.question_raw: A gym charges a one-time registration fee of $25 plus $40 per month. If Marcus has budgeted $345 for gym expenses, which equation can be used to find the maximum number of months, m, he can afford?

hints.text: Identify the fixed cost (one-time fee) and the variable cost (monthly charge). The total budget equals the sum of both.
The total cost is: registration fee + (monthly fee × number of months) = $345. That's \(25 + 40m = 345\).

solution_steps.text: The one-time registration fee is $25 (constant term).
The monthly fee is $40 per month, so for \(m\) months the variable cost is \(40m\).

problems.question_html: If \(3(2x - 5) = 4x + 7\), what is the value of \(x\)?
problems.question_raw: If 3(2x - 5) = 4x + 7, what is the value of x?

hints.text: Start by distributing the 3 on the left side: \(3 \cdot 2x - 3 \cdot 5\).
After distributing: \(6x - 15 = 4x + 7\). Now isolate \(x\) by subtracting \(4x\) from both sides.

problems.question_html: Which of the following values of \(x\) satisfies the inequality \(-3x + 7 > 19\)?
problems.question_raw: Which of the following values of x satisfies the inequality -3x + 7 > 19?

hints.text: Solve the inequality step by step. Remember: when you divide by a negative number, flip the inequality sign.
\(-3x + 7 > 19\) → \(-3x > 12\) → \(x < -4\). Which answer choice is less than \(-4\)?

solution_steps.text: Divide both sides by \(-3\) and flip the sign: \(x < -4\).

problems.question_html: For what value of \(a\) does the equation \(a(x + 3) = 4x + 12\) have infinitely many solutions?
problems.question_raw: For what value of a does the equation a(x + 3) = 4x + 12 have infinitely many solutions?

hints.text: For infinitely many solutions, both sides must be identical after simplifying. Distribute \(a\) on the left side and compare coefficients.
Left side: \(ax + 3a\). Right side: \(4x + 12\). For these to be identical: \(a = 4\) and \(3a = 12\). Both give \(a = 4\).

solution_steps.text: For infinitely many solutions, coefficients must match: \(a = 4\) (from the \(x\) terms).

problems.question_html: If \(|2x - 5| = 11\), what is the sum of all possible values of \(x\)?
problems.question_raw: If |2x - 5| = 11, what is the sum of all possible values of x?

hints.text: An absolute value equation \(|A| = B\) splits into two cases: \(A = B\) or \(A = -B\).
Case 1: \(2x - 5 = 11 \Rightarrow x = 8\). Case 2: \(2x - 5 = -11 \Rightarrow x = -3\). Find the sum.

problems.question_html: A phone plan charges $0.10 per text message and $0.05 per minute of talk time. Last month, Priya sent 3 times as many text messages as minutes she talked. If her total bill was $17.50, how many text messages did she send?
problems.question_raw: A phone plan charges $0.10 per text message and $0.05 per minute of talk time. Last month, Priya sent 3 times as many text messages as minutes she talked. If her total bill was $17.50, how many text messages did she send?

hints.text: Let \(t\) = minutes talked. Then text messages = \(3t\). Write the cost equation: \(0.10(3t) + 0.05(t) = 17.50\).

concepts.title: Systems of Linear Equations
concepts.content_html:
Systems of Linear Equations on the SAT

A system of linear equations consists of two or more equations with two or more unknowns. The SAT frequently tests your ability to solve these systems and interpret the solutions in real-world contexts.

Key Concept
A system of two linear equations in two variables can have:
• One solution — the lines intersect at exactly one point
• No solution — the lines are parallel (same slope, different intercepts)
• Infinitely many solutions — the lines are the same (identical equations)

Method 1: Substitution

Best when one variable is already isolated or has a coefficient of 1:

  1. Solve one equation for one variable
  2. Substitute that expression into the other equation
  3. Solve and back-substitute

Method 2: Elimination (Combination)

Best when both equations are in standard form \(ax + by = c\):

  1. Multiply one or both equations so that one variable's coefficients are opposites
  2. Add the equations to eliminate that variable
  3. Solve and back-substitute
SAT Tip
The SAT often asks for an expression like \(x + y\) or \(2x - y\) rather than individual values. Before solving for each variable separately, check whether adding or subtracting the given equations directly yields the desired expression — this can save significant time.

No Solution vs. Infinitely Many

For the system \(a_1x + b_1y = c_1\) and \(a_2x + b_2y = c_2\):

  • No solution: \(\dfrac{a_1}{a_2} = \dfrac{b_1}{b_2} \neq \dfrac{c_1}{c_2}\) (parallel lines)
  • Infinitely many: \(\dfrac{a_1}{a_2} = \dfrac{b_1}{b_2} = \dfrac{c_1}{c_2}\) (same line)
Watch Out!
When the SAT asks "for what value of \(k\) does the system have no solution," you're being asked to make the lines parallel. Set the slope ratio equal but the constant ratio different.
concepts.content_raw: Systems of Linear Equations on the SAT

problems.question_html: If \(x + y = 10\) and \(3x - y = 14\), what is the value of \(x\)?
problems.question_raw: If x + y = 10 and 3x - y = 14, what is the value of x?

hints.text: Try the elimination method: adding the two equations will eliminate \(y\).

solution_steps.text: Add the two equations: \(x + y + 3x - y = 10 + 14\).

problems.question_html: A coffee shop sells lattes for $5 and cappuccinos for $4. On Monday, the shop sold a total of 80 drinks and earned $350. How many lattes were sold?
problems.question_raw: A coffee shop sells lattes for $5 and cappuccinos for $4. On Monday, the shop sold a total of 80 drinks and earned $350. How many lattes were sold?

hints.text: Set up two equations. Let \(L\) = lattes and \(C\) = cappuccinos. Use the total drinks and total revenue.
System: \(L + C = 80\) and \(5L + 4C = 350\). Solve the first for \(C = 80 - L\) and substitute.

problems.question_html:
For what value of \(k\) does the following system have no solution?

\[3x + 6y = 12\]

\[kx + 2y = 5\]
problems.question_raw: For what value of k does the system 3x + 6y = 12 and kx + 2y = 5 have no solution?

hints.text: For no solution, the lines must be parallel: same slope but different y-intercepts. Compare the ratios of the coefficients.
Rewrite the first equation: divide by 3 → \(x + 2y = 4\). For parallel lines, we need \(k = 1\) so both have the form \(x + 2y = \text{something}\), but \(4 \neq 5\).

solution_steps.text: For no solution, the ratio of coefficients must be equal but the constant ratio must differ.
Check constants: \(\dfrac{12}{5} \neq 3\) ✓ (so no solution, not infinitely many).

problems.question_html: If \(2x + 3y = 12\) and \(4x - 3y = 6\), what is the value of \(x + y\)?
problems.question_raw: If 2x + 3y = 12 and 4x - 3y = 6, what is the value of x + y?

hints.text: Before solving for \(x\) and \(y\) individually, try adding the equations. Does either operation give you \(x + y\) directly?

problems.question_html: Tickets to a school play cost $8 for adults and $5 for students. A total of 200 tickets were sold, and the revenue was $1,180. How many student tickets were sold?
problems.question_raw: Tickets to a school play cost $8 for adults and $5 for students. A total of 200 tickets were sold, and the revenue was $1,180. How many student tickets were sold?

hints.text: Let \(a\) = adult tickets and \(s\) = student tickets. Write two equations: one for total tickets, one for revenue.
\(a + s = 200\) and \(8a + 5s = 1180\). From the first: \(a = 200 - s\). Substitute into the second.

problems.question_html:
The system of equations below has infinitely many solutions. What is the value of \(b\)?

\[4x - 2y = 10\]

\[-6x + 3y = b\]
problems.question_raw: The system 4x - 2y = 10 and -6x + 3y = b has infinitely many solutions. What is the value of b?

hints.text: For infinitely many solutions, one equation must be a scalar multiple of the other. What do you multiply the first equation by to match the second?
Multiply equation 1 by \(-\frac{3}{2}\): \(4 \cdot (-\frac{3}{2}) = -6\) ✓ and \(-2 \cdot (-\frac{3}{2}) = 3\) ✓. So \(b = 10 \cdot (-\frac{3}{2}) = -15\).

solution_steps.text: For infinitely many solutions, the second equation must be a multiple of the first.

problems.question_html: At a farmers' market, Elena buys 3 pounds of apples and 2 pounds of oranges for $11.50. Raj buys 1 pound of apples and 4 pounds of oranges for $10.50. What is the price per pound of apples?
problems.question_raw: Elena buys 3 pounds of apples and 2 pounds of oranges for $11.50. Raj buys 1 pound of apples and 4 pounds of oranges for $10.50. What is the price per pound of apples?

hints.text: Set up the system: \(3a + 2o = 11.50\) and \(a + 4o = 10.50\). Use elimination — multiply the second equation to eliminate \(a\).
Multiply equation 2 by 3: \(3a + 12o = 31.50\). Subtract equation 1: \(10o = 20\) → \(o = 2\). Then \(a + 8 = 10.50\) → \(a = 2.50\).

problems.question_html: If \(x - 2y = 5\) and \(3x + 2y = 19\), what is the value of \(2x - y\)?
problems.question_raw: If x - 2y = 5 and 3x + 2y = 19, what is the value of 2x - y?

hints.text: First solve the system by adding the equations (the \(y\) terms cancel). Then compute \(2x - y\).

concepts.title: Linear Functions and Graphs
concepts.content_html:
Linear Functions and Graphs on the SAT

The SAT tests your understanding of linear functions — their equations, graphs, and real-world interpretations. You need to move fluently between tables, equations, graphs, and word descriptions.

Key Concept
The slope-intercept form of a linear function is \(f(x) = mx + b\), where:
• \(m\) = slope (rate of change) — how much \(y\) changes for each unit increase in \(x\)
• \(b\) = y-intercept — the value of \(f(x)\) when \(x = 0\) (the starting value)

Slope Formula

The slope between two points \((x_1, y_1)\) and \((x_2, y_2)\) is:

\[m = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}\]

Interpreting Slope and Intercept in Context

The SAT frequently asks you to interpret slope and y-intercept in real-world scenarios:

  • Slope = the rate of change — "for each additional [unit of x], the [quantity y] increases/decreases by [m]"
  • y-intercept = the initial value — "when [x] is zero, the [quantity y] is [b]"
SAT Tip
When asked to interpret slope or y-intercept, always include the units and context. For example: "The slope of 15 means the cost increases by $15 per hour." Wrong answers often have correct numbers but wrong units or wrong direction (increase vs. decrease).

Parallel and Perpendicular Lines

  • Parallel lines: same slope, different y-intercepts (\(m_1 = m_2\))
  • Perpendicular lines: slopes are negative reciprocals (\(m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1\))

Finding the Equation of a Line

Given a point \((x_1, y_1)\) and slope \(m\), use point-slope form:

\[y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)\]

Watch Out!
Don't confuse rate of change (slope) with total change. If a question says "increased by $120 over 8 hours," the slope is \(\frac{120}{8} = 15\) per hour, not 120.
concepts.content_raw: Linear Functions and Graphs on the SAT

problems.question_html: A plumber charges a flat service fee of $75 plus $50 per hour of labor. Which function \(C(h)\) represents the total cost for \(h\) hours of labor?
problems.question_raw: A plumber charges a flat service fee of $75 plus $50 per hour. Which function C(h) represents the total cost for h hours?

hints.text: In \(C(h) = mh + b\), what is the rate (per hour) and what is the starting value (flat fee)?
Rate = $50/hour → coefficient of \(h\). Flat fee = $75 → constant. So \(C(h) = 50h + 75\).

solution_steps.text: The flat fee ($75) is the y-intercept — it doesn't depend on hours.
The hourly rate ($50) is the slope — it multiplies \(h\).

problems.question_html: The equation \(y = -2x + 100\) models the number of pages remaining in a book after \(x\) minutes of reading. What does the slope \(-2\) represent in this context?
problems.question_raw: The equation y = -2x + 100 models pages remaining after x minutes. What does the slope -2 represent?

choices.text: The reader finishes 2 pages per minute
The reader has read 2 pages total
The book will be finished in 2 minutes

hints.text: The slope represents the rate of change. The negative sign tells you the pages remaining are decreasing.
Slope = \(-2\) means for each additional minute (\(x\)), the pages remaining (\(y\)) decrease by 2. The reader finishes 2 pages per minute.

solution_steps.text: The slope \(m = -2\) represents the rate of change of \(y\) with respect to \(x\).
For each 1-minute increase in reading time, the number of remaining pages decreases by 2.
In context: the reader finishes 2 pages per minute. The answer is B.

problems.question_html: Line \(\ell\) passes through the points \((2, 5)\) and \((6, 13)\). Which of the following is the equation of a line parallel to \(\ell\)?
problems.question_raw: Line l passes through (2, 5) and (6, 13). Which equation is parallel to l?

hints.text: First find the slope of line \(\ell\). Parallel lines have the same slope.
Slope of \(\ell\): \(\frac{13-5}{6-2} = \frac{8}{4} = 2\). A parallel line also has slope 2.

solution_steps.text: Parallel lines have identical slopes, so we need slope = 2.
Only \(y = 2x + 1\) has slope 2. The answer is B.

problems.question_html:
The table below shows the relationship between hours worked and total earnings:

Hours (\(h\))Earnings ($)
020
356
580
8116

What does the value 20 represent in this relationship?
problems.question_raw: A table shows Hours: 0,3,5,8 and Earnings: $20,$56,$80,$116. What does the value 20 represent?

choices.text: A signing bonus or base pay
The number of hours worked for free

hints.text: When \(h = 0\), earnings = $20. What does it mean to earn money before working any hours?
The y-intercept (value at \(h = 0\)) represents a fixed amount earned regardless of hours — like a signing bonus or base pay.

solution_steps.text: At \(h = 0\), earnings = $20. This is the y-intercept.
The y-intercept represents the starting value — earnings before any hours are worked.
This is a fixed payment like a signing bonus or base pay. The answer is B.

problems.question_html: Line \(p\) has the equation \(y = 3x - 7\). Line \(q\) is perpendicular to line \(p\) and passes through the point \((6, 1)\). What is the y-intercept of line \(q\)?
problems.question_raw: Line p: y = 3x - 7. Line q is perpendicular to p and passes through (6, 1). What is the y-intercept of q?

hints.text: Perpendicular lines have slopes that are negative reciprocals. If line \(p\) has slope 3, what is the slope of line \(q\)?
Slope of \(q\) = \(-\frac{1}{3}\). Use point-slope with \((6, 1)\): \(y - 1 = -\frac{1}{3}(x - 6)\). Simplify to find the y-intercept.

solution_steps.text: Slope of \(p\) is 3, so slope of \(q\) is \(-\frac{1}{3}\) (negative reciprocal).

problems.question_html: A scatterplot shows a linear trend. The line of best fit passes through approximately \((10, 40)\) and \((30, 120)\). Based on this line, what is the predicted value of \(y\) when \(x = 20\)?
problems.question_raw: A line of best fit passes through (10, 40) and (30, 120). What is the predicted y when x = 20?

hints.text: Find the slope using the two given points. Notice that \(x = 20\) is exactly the midpoint of \(x = 10\) and \(x = 30\).
Since \(x = 20\) is the midpoint, \(y\) is the average of the \(y\)-values: \(\frac{40 + 120}{2} = 80\).

solution_steps.text: Since \(x = 20\) is the midpoint of 10 and 30, the predicted \(y\) is the midpoint of 40 and 120.
